turns.content: Okay, let's dig into the Customer Feedback doc. I want to see how we're perceived against the competition. Can you pull up the competitive perception data? I want to make sure our messaging is aligned with what customers are actually saying.

Certainly. The `Customer_Feedback.pdf`, Section 6, 'Competitive Perception', provides a table comparing Ficduct against two key competitors based on survey responses. Ficduct received a score of 4.3 out of 5 for 'Ease of Setup' compared to 2.9 and 3.4 for competitors. For 'Real-Time Capabilities', Ficduct scored 4.6, while competitors scored 3.1 and 2.8.

This is fantastic. We're crushing them on ease of setup and our core real-time differentiator. And look at 'Pricing Transparency'—a 4.4 versus their 2.5! This is a marketer's dream and directly validates our messaging pillars from the Marketing & Sales doc.

Yes, the data in Section 6 of `Customer_Feedback.pdf` shows strong performance in those areas. The same table also indicates areas where competitors were rated higher. For 'Documentation Quality', Competitor A scored 4.1 versus Ficduct's 3.7. For 'Enterprise Feature Set', Competitor A scored 4.5 versus Ficduct's 3.9.

Right, but that higher 'Enterprise Feature Set' score for Competitor A is expected. They're the bloated incumbent, likely MuleSoft. Our entire brand positioning is about being the nimble, modern platform that's easier to use. The higher 'Ease of Setup' score proves that our wedge strategy is working perfectly.

That's a valid strategic interpretation. It is worth noting, however, that the 'Churn Analysis' in Section 7 of the `Customer_Feedback.pdf` links this feature gap to a lost customer. Modo Analytics, with an ARR of $72K, churned because they 'needed real-time CDC for Oracle; not yet supported.' This is categorized as a 'Yes (roadmap item)' for preventability.

Okay, that's one actionable loss, and it's on the roadmap. But let's not lose sight of the forest for the trees. Our overall customer sentiment is incredibly strong. What's the latest overall NPS data from the Customer Feedback doc?

According to Section 2 of the `Customer_Feedback.pdf`, Ficduct's current NPS for Q1 2026 is 62. This is an increase from 59 in Q4 2025 and 48 at the time of General Availability in Q2 2025. The report notes this score is in the 'Excellent' category.

An NPS of 62 is world-class for B2B SaaS and shows a fantastic trend. I remember seeing a breakdown by customer segment though, which might explain some of the friction in our PLG funnel. Can you pull the NPS by segment?

Yes. Section 2.1, 'NPS by Segment', in the `Customer_Feedback.pdf` provides that breakdown. Enterprise customers report an NPS of 71, Professional-tier customers report 58, and Starter-tier customers report 49. The document's analysis states, 'The gap between tiers suggests that the self-service experience needs improvement to match the quality of the managed experience.'

That 49 NPS for the Starter tier is exactly what I was talking about in our last session. It's the source of our 52% MQL-to-SQL conversion problem. The `V2 Planning` doc has a plan to fix this, doesn't it? What are the planned improvements for the self-service onboarding experience?

Correct. The `V2 Planning` document, in Section 4.3 'Onboarding Experience', details the plan. Key features include an interactive tutorial to guide new users, pre-built templates for common use cases, an in-app help overlay with contextual tips, and a sandbox environment with pre-loaded synthetic data to eliminate the need to connect real systems during evaluation.

This is exactly what we need. The goal of a 'first working integration within 30 minutes' mentioned in the V2 Design Principles is a powerful marketing claim. This will directly improve that Starter tier NPS and our funnel conversion. We should budget a major campaign around this 'Zero-Friction Onboarding' for the V2 launch.

That aligns with the V2 objectives. Regarding budget, it's useful to consider the current financial context. The `Financial_Report.pdf`, in Section 3 'Cost Structure', shows that Sales & Marketing spend for Q1 2026 was $1,540K, which represents 41% of total revenue.

And worth every penny! Our unit economics are phenomenal. The `Financial_Report` itself shows an 8.2:1 LTV:CAC ratio in Section 4. Our campaigns are highly efficient; the `Marketing_Sales.pdf` (Section 5) shows our Integration Summit webinar had a CPL of just $14.92. This is the time to pour fuel on the fire, not worry about the budget.

The unit economics are indeed strong. To provide a complete picture, the `Financial_Report.pdf` also highlights the overall company financials. Section 1.1 shows a Q1 2026 Operating Loss of ($1.1M), and Section 5, 'Cash Flow & Burn Rate', reports a monthly net burn of approximately $367K. The company is projected to reach breakeven by Q1 2027.

I understand we have a burn rate; we're a venture-backed growth company in a market projected to hit $13.9 billion. The `Strategy & Operations` doc explicitly lists 'Scalable Growth' and 'Market Expansion' as two of our four strategic pillars. We can't hit our $52M ARR target for FY 2027 by pulling back on the growth engine.

You are correct. Section 1 of the `Strategy & Operations.pdf` identifies 'Scalable Growth' as a pillar with a key result of reaching $52M ARR by the end of FY 2027. Furthermore, Section 1.1, 'Strategic Trade-offs', notes that the company is 'accepting a slower path to profitability in exchange for faster international expansion,' which aligns with your point about investing in growth.

Exactly. A deliberate strategic trade-off. It's about smart investment in growth, which is what my team delivers. Now, shifting back to our competitive strengths—I remember seeing a great customer quote in the feedback doc praising our AI-assisted mapping. Can you find that for me? It's a perfect proof point against the legacy players.

Of course. That testimonial is in the `Customer_Feedback.pdf`, Section 5, 'Customer Testimonials'. It's from an Integration Architect at Meridian Health, who said: 'The AI-assisted mapping saved our team at least 40 hours on the initial setup. It correctly mapped 85% of fields on the first try.'

Perfect. That quote is gold. It quantifies the value prop. This is the kind of material we need to amplify. Speaking of which, what does the Customer Feedback doc say are the most requested features? I want to see if they align with the big bets in V2.

The `Customer_Feedback.pdf` lists the most requested features in a table in Section 4.1. The top three are: 'Custom dashboard builder in Insight' with 47 mentions, 'Webhook debugging / replay tool' with 38 mentions, and 'Multi-environment flow promotion' with 34 mentions.

That's great alignment. The V2 plan is heavily focused on the Insight overhaul and multi-environment promotion. This confirms the product team is listening. What's the satisfaction score for the Insight module specifically? I recall it being a weak spot.

You're correct, it is the lowest-rated module. According to the 'Satisfaction by Product Area' table in Section 3 of `Customer_Feedback.pdf`, Ficduct Insight has a satisfaction score of 3.6 out of 5. The trend shows it has declined by 0.1 points, and the document notes this is 'the most concerning trend.'

A declining score is a problem, but it's also an opportunity. V2's natural language querying for Insight will be a huge story. The Customer Advisory Board had specific feedback on this, right? What was their take?

Yes, they did. The `Customer_Feedback.pdf`, Section 8, 'Customer Advisory Board', notes that in the inaugural meeting, the CAB provided a 'request to prioritize natural-language querying in Insight over additional chart types.' This feedback directly influenced the V2 roadmap.

Excellent. This is how you build a competitive moat—by listening to your best customers. It also gives us a powerful co-marketing opportunity with those CAB members when we launch V2. Now, let's look at churn from a marketing perspective. What were the reasons for the three churns listed in the analysis?

The `Customer_Feedback.pdf`, Section 7, details the three churns. TechStart Inc. ($36K ARR) was acquired by a company using a competitor. GreenLeaf Logistics ($48K ARR) had a budget cut and eliminated their integration team. Modo Analytics ($72K ARR) churned due to the lack of a specific feature, Oracle CDC.

So one was unpreventable, one was a feature gap we're closing, and one was a budget cut. The GreenLeaf one is interesting. The doc says it was 'Partially' preventable. Why? This sounds like a risk our messaging needs to address.

The analysis for the GreenLeaf churn in Section 7 of `Customer_Feedback.pdf` states: 'The churn highlights the importance of multi-stakeholder engagement — the integration team was the sole champion, and when it was dissolved, there was no executive sponsor to advocate for renewal.' This suggests the value of Ficduct wasn't successfully communicated to the broader business leadership.

That's a key insight for my team. Our messaging needs to be broader than just the technical buyer. We need to be arming our champions with the business value and ROI story to sell upwards. This ties back to the ROI calculator we built, which the Marketing & Sales doc says was a successful asset.

Yes, the 'Content Performance' table in Section 6.1 of the `Marketing & Sales.pdf` lists the 'Integration ROI Calculator' as an interactive tool that received 1,900 views and generated 210 leads, indicating its effectiveness as a top-of-funnel asset.

Good. We should probably double down on content like that. While we're on marketing assets, what does the Marketing & Sales doc say about our 'Ficduct vs. MuleSoft' comparison guide? How did that perform?

That asset also performed well. The same 'Content Performance' table in the `Marketing & Sales.pdf` (Section 6.1) shows the 'Ficduct vs. MuleSoft: An Honest Comparison' guide had 3,100 views and generated 190 leads.

3,100 views is strong. That tells me there's a huge appetite for that direct comparison. It reinforces that our positioning against them as a more modern, agile alternative is resonating in the market. Now, what's the plan for our own conference? I saw a mention of FicConnect.

The `Marketing_Sales.pdf` lists 'Customer Conference (FicConnect 2026)' in the Marketing Roadmap (Section 9). The event is planned for September 2026 with an objective to host '200+ attendees' and establish it as a 'brand anchor event'.

A brand anchor event in September is perfect timing to build hype for the V2 launch in Q1 2027. We can use it to beta test messaging and showcase the new Flow Builder and Insight module. This all seems well-aligned. Let's look at the sales pipeline. Is there still concentration risk in the enterprise segment?

Yes, that risk is still present. The 'Pipeline Analysis' in Section 4.2 of the `Marketing_Sales.pdf` notes a concentration risk in the enterprise pipeline. It states that 'the three largest enterprise deals in negotiation represent $3.8M of the $6.1M weighted value at that stage.' The Financial Report (Section 2.1) corroborates this, noting the top three customers contribute 31% of total ARR.

Okay, that's a consistent theme. Broadening the top of the enterprise funnel needs to be a Q3 marketing priority to support sales. We can use the upcoming Gartner Magic Quadrant submission mentioned in the marketing roadmap to drive awareness with a new set of enterprise targets. This has been a very productive review.

Indeed. The `Marketing_Sales.pdf` roadmap in Section 9 lists the 'Gartner Magic Quadrant Submission' for Q2 2026 with the objective to 'Achieve “Visionary” positioning'. This aligns with your strategy to leverage analyst relations to broaden enterprise awareness.
